Agricultural Economist · Researcher

Martin Sato

Exploring how behaviour shapes food systems

I study the intersection of economics, human behaviour, and food safety in developing contexts. Currently an Erasmus Mundus scholar at Ghent University, I work on understanding why small-scale producers and vendors adopt (or resist) food-safety practices—and what that means for policy.

Say Hello LinkedIn GitHub
About

A Bit About Me

My academic path has taken me from Kenya to China to Europe. I hold a BSc from Karatina University and an MSc from the Chinese Academy of Agricultural Sciences, and I am currently completing the Erasmus Mundus International Master in Rural Development, splitting time between Ghent, Slovakia, and France.

Before returning to academia, I spent nearly three years as an Agricultural Economist with the Kisii County Government, where I led field-level value-chain analyses, built data dashboards, and helped secure grant funding for smallholder programmes. Earlier, I interned at World Agroforestry (ICRAF) in Nairobi, working on post-harvest loss reduction and agroforestry economics.

My current research uses an extended Theory of Planned Behaviour framework to examine food-safety practice adoption among umqombothi vendors in South Africa’s informal markets. I’m broadly interested in how behavioural economics can inform better food-system policies in the Global South.

Selected Work

Current Thesis

Food-Safety Practice Adoption Among Umqombothi Vendors

Applying an extended Theory of Planned Behaviour in South Africa’s informal markets — Ghent University, 2025–26
Journal Article

Determinants of Kenyan Tea Exports: The Gravity Model Approach

Journal of Economic and Sustainable Development, Vol. 11, No. 14 (2020) — DOI 10.7176/JESD/10-14-15
Martin Ndemo Sato
Abstract: Tea exports are one of the primary drivers of economic growth in Kenya. This paper examined the key factors determining Kenya’s tea exports to its major 15 trading partners for the period 1990 to 2017 using a gravity model method. The main findings suggest that an increase in the economic size of the importing country and depreciation of the Kenyan shilling increases tea exports, while an increase in population and per capita GDP of the importing country decreases demand. Distance has a negative impact on exports. Sharing a common border, trading with non-landlocked countries, sharing a common colonial history, and COMESA membership all positively influence tea export flows. These results are essential for the formulation of trade policy to ensure Kenya’s tea export potential is fully exploited.

R Data Analysis Project

Kenya Census 2019: Population Distribution Analysis

Interactive exploration of Kenya’s 2019 Population and Housing Census across all 47 counties

A comprehensive analysis of Kenya’s 2019 census data covering 47 counties and 346 sub-counties. Click the tabs below to explore population distribution, gender composition, population density, and drill down into individual counties.
